summary: 13th-century English Franciscan friar known as Doctor Mirabilis, author of the Opus Maius. Associated with early advocacy of empiricism and experimental science, the first European written formula for gunpowder, optics (Perspectiva), semiotics, and the legend of the brazen talking head.

works.name: Opus Maius
works.description: Roger Bacon's best-known work, sent to Pope Clement IV, urging a reformed curriculum covering 'natural philosophy' and 'experimental science.' It contains sections on 'The Four General Causes of Human Ignorance,' semiotics, and optics (the Perspectiva). It also contains the first written formula for gunpowder in Europe, recommending charcoal from willow trees. The work connects Bacon's interest in natural phenomena with the understanding of Scripture.

clues.clue: Sent to Pope Clement IV; urges reformed curriculum covering 'natural philosophy' and 'experimental science'
examples: wrote on 'The Four General Causes of Human Ignorance' in a volume sent to Pope Clement IV which urges a reformed curriculum covering 'natural philosophy' and 'experimental science.'

clues.clue: Contains first European written formula for gunpowder (recommends charcoal from willow trees)
examples: This philosopher's Opus Majus contains the first written formula for gunpowder in Europe
Charcoal from willow trees is recommended in a recipe for gunpowder in this monk's Opus Maius.

clues.clue: Connects interest in natural phenomena with understanding of Scripture
examples: connects this thinker's interest in natural phenomena with the understanding of Scripture

works.name: Other Works and Language Theory
works.description: Bacon's early work on language, the Summa Grammatica, drew on Robert Kilwardby's commentaries on Priscian. His De signis expanded on Augustine's theory of linguistic signs as triadic relations. His idea that a common grammar underlies all languages influenced the Modist school of speculative grammarians. He also wrote a seven-chapter commentary on the Secretum Secretorum.

clues.clue: De signis expanded on Augustine's theory of linguistic signs as triadic relations
examples: A medieval philosopher with this surname expanded on Augustine's theory of linguistic signs as triadic relations in his work De signis.

clues.clue: His idea of a common grammar underlying all languages influenced the Modist school (Thomas of Erfurt)
examples: Roger Bacon's idea that a common grammar underlies all languages influenced this school of philosophers across northern Europe, whose most famous member was Thomas of Erfurt.

clues.clue: Summa Grammatica drew on Robert Kilwardby's commentaries on Priscian
examples: This thinker drew heavily on Robert Kilwardby's commentaries on Priscian for his early work on language, the Summa Grammatica.

clues.clue: Commentary on Secretum Secretorum (text purportedly by Aristotle for Alexander the Great)
examples: This thinker's political philosophy is contained in his seven-chapter-long commentary on the Sirr-al-'asrar, also known as the Secretum secretorum
This author of the Opus Majus produced an annotated edition of the Secretum Secretorum.

clues.clue: Communia naturalium argued the intellectual soul was created externally, not together with vegetative soul
examples: This philosopher wrote a polemic against those who think that vegetative and intellectual souls were created together

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Known as Doctor Mirabilis. English 13th-century Franciscan monk. His main intellectual legacy was advocating empiricism and experimental science. A legend credits him with creating a brazen (brass) talking head that could answer any question. This legend was popularized in Robert Greene's Elizabethan comedy Friar Bacon and Friar Bungay, where the head's only utterances are 'TIME is,' 'TIME was,' and 'TIME is PAST.' Daniel Defoe mentioned the brazen head in Journal of the Plague Year.

clues.clue: Known as Doctor Mirabilis; 13th-century English Franciscan monk
examples: identify this 13th century author of the Opus Maius, who was known as Doctor Mirabilis
Name this Doctor Mirabilis

examples: This philosopher's main intellectual legacy was advocating empiricism.

clues.clue: Legend of brazen talking head; popularized in Robert Greene's Friar Bacon and Friar Bungay ('TIME is,' 'TIME was,' 'TIME is PAST')
examples: was said to have created a brazen talking head that could answer any question
In a comic 1590 play, the incompetence of a servant named Miles leads a scholar who has spent seven years of study to miss those prophetic lines

clues.clue: Daniel Defoe mentioned Bacon's brazen head in Journal of the Plague Year
examples: This author wrote that Bacon's brazen head marked signs for fortune tellers that sprang up in the wake of a 1665 epidemic in his Journal of the Plague Year

clues.clue: Shares surname with Francis Bacon (Novum Organum, Idols of the Mind, scientific method)

A later philosopher with this surname advocated the scientific method in his Novum Organum.

comprehensive_summary:
Roger Bacon is a 13th-century English Franciscan friar known as Doctor Mirabilis. His best-known work, the Opus Maius, was sent to Pope Clement IV and urges a reformed curriculum covering 'natural philosophy' and 'experimental science.' It discusses 'The Four General Causes of Human Ignorance' and contains sections on semiotics and optics (the Perspectiva). The Opus Maius also contains the first written formula for gunpowder in Europe, recommending charcoal from willow trees. His main intellectual legacy was advocating empiricism.

Bacon's work on language was also significant. His De signis expanded on Augustine's theory of linguistic signs as triadic relations, and his idea that a common grammar underlies all languages influenced the Modist school of speculative grammarians, whose most famous member was Thomas of Erfurt. His early work, the Summa Grammatica, drew on Robert Kilwardby's commentaries on Priscian. He also wrote a seven-chapter commentary on the Secretum Secretorum, a text purportedly by Aristotle for Alexander the Great. Robert Grosseteste was his teacher.

A famous legend credits Bacon with creating a brazen (brass) talking head that could answer any question. This legend was popularized in Robert Greene's Elizabethan comedy Friar Bacon and Friar Bungay, where the head's only utterances are 'TIME is,' 'TIME was,' and 'TIME is PAST' before being destroyed when the servant Miles fails to wake Bacon. Daniel Defoe mentioned the brazen head in his Journal of the Plague Year.

Bacon frequently shares clue space with the later Francis Bacon (author of Novum Organum and the 'four idols of the mind'), and questions sometimes ask for the shared surname 'Bacon.'

Work: contains the first written formula for gunpowder in Europe, recommending charcoal from willow trees
Philosopher: his De signis expanded on Augustine's theory of linguistic signs as triadic relations, and his idea of a common grammar underlying all languages influenced the Modist school
Philosopher: wrote a seven-chapter commentary on the Secretum Secretorum, a text purportedly authored by Aristotle for Alexander the Great
Philosopher: a legend credits this Doctor Mirabilis with creating a brazen talking head; Robert Greene's play Friar Bacon and Friar Bungay has it say 'TIME is,' 'TIME was,' 'TIME is PAST'
